docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: This is the events that happened on Friday, January 9th, 2026 in ACIT 2811 - UX/UI. The instructions of in the class was to find three examples of websites with bad user interfaces. We were to pick one out of the three to redesign.

docs.title: Websites
docs.text: The following were our top three websites 1. Discount beds 2. Yale school of Art 3. X-ray

docs.title: Chosen Website
docs.text: The website we chose was discount beds. image fo discount beds

docs.title: Reasons
docs.text:
We chose the discount beds website for the following reasons.

  1. Needs a good Structure
  2. International freelancing opportunities

docs.title: Redesign Details
docs.text:
• Navigations bar
  • Multiple webpages
  • Contact page
  • Payment page
  • Login page
  • Pictures for available beds in stock

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: This is the report of the event that took place in ACIT 2811 - UX/UI on January 16th, 2026. The class was a simple introduction to marketing researching. We had two in class activities and a take home proposal assignment.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text: Three main topics were discussed in class, 1. Design Thinking 2. User-Centered Design 3. Usability Factors

docs.title: Definition of Topics
docs.text:
• Design Thinking is a problem solving process that can be applied to problems.

  • User-Centered Design is an iterative set of steps to solve problems related to a product or service for users.

docs.title: Design Thinking process
docs.text:
1. Empthize
  2. Define
  3. Ideate - brainstorm
  4. Prototype
  5. Test

docs.title: User-Centered Design process
docs.text:
1. Understand the context
  2. Specify requirements
  3. Design solutions
  4. Evaluate solutions

docs.title: In-class activities
docs.text:
These are the in class activities we did in class. The first one involved design thinking. We were given a case study and asked to write a Design thinking process for it. This was done individually.

For the second activity each group in class was given a criteria (Ours: Accessible and not Valuable; and vice versa) and asked to find two products that met the criteria. The second activity was done in a group. Our group consisted of three individual: * Ryan Bo * Long * Calvin

  1. Design Thinking in class activity
  2. 7 Factors Group Activity

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text:
• Project Proposal
  • Market Research Document

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: This is a report that took place in ACIT 2811 - UX/UI on _January 24th, 2026. The class involved a lot of activites. These activities were conducted after Chris explain each topic.

docs.title: Topics / in-class-activies
docs.text: The topics and in-class activities focused on using personas, user scenarios, and user stories to better understand user needs and guide design decisions.

docs.title: Definition of terms
docs.text:
• Personas: identify user characteristics, behaviours, and goals

  • User Scenarios: a technical description of the steps required to complete one of your user stories.

  • User Stories: identify features (technical features of a product/service that need to be built). It make up a list of tasks that you can use to ensure, at the end of the project, that you have built everything you set out to build

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: This is the report of the event that took place in ACIT 2811 - UX/UI. The class focused on developing problem statements, generating ideas, and creating supporting artifacts. We also worked on defining features for potential solutions through structured activities.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
The main topics discussed in class were:

  1. Problem Statements
  2. Ideation
  3. Artifacts
  4. Feature Lists

docs.title: Definition of Topics
docs.text:
• Problem Statements clearly define the issue being addressed, focusing on the user, their needs, and the context of the problem.
  • Ideation is the process of generating a wide range of ideas and possible solutions, often through brainstorming techniques.
  • Artifacts are tangible outputs (such as sketches, diagrams, or notes) that represent ideas and help communicate design thinking.
  • Feature Lists outline the key functionalities or characteristics that a product or solution should include.

docs.title: Problem Statement Structure
docs.text:
A well-formed problem statement typically includes:

  1. The target user
  2. The user’s need or goal
  3. The problem or pain point

docs.title: Ideation Process
docs.text:
1. Brainstorm ideas freely
  2. Explore multiple solutions
  3. Refine and select promising ideas

docs.title: In-class Activities
docs.text:
These are the in-class activities completed during the session.

The first activity focused on creating a clear problem statement based on a given scenario. This helped us better understand how to define user needs and frame problems effectively. This activity was completed individually.

The second activity involved ideation, where we brainstormed multiple solutions to the defined problem and created artifacts to represent our ideas. These artifacts included sketches and notes to visualize potential solutions.

The final part of the activity required us to develop a feature list based on our chosen idea, identifying the most important functionalities of the proposed solution.

This activity was completed in a group. Our group consisted of:

Ryan Bo Long Calvin

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text: Problem Statement Development Feature List Creation Supporting Design Artifacts

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: This is the report of the event that took place in ACIT 2811 - UX/UI. The class focused on Information Architecture, exploring how content is structured and organized to improve user experience. We learned how clear organization, navigation, and labeling help users find information efficiently.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
The main topics discussed in class were:

  1. Content Hierarchy
  2. Navigation Systems
  3. Labelling Systems
  4. Artifacts
  5. Sitemap

docs.title: Definition of Topics
docs.text:
• Content Hierarchy refers to the organization and prioritization of information in a way that guides users through content logically and efficiently.
  • Navigation Systems are the structures that allow users to move through a product or website, helping them find information quickly and easily.
  • Labelling Systems involve the use of clear and consistent names for categories, menus, and content so users can easily understand what each section represents.
  • Artifacts are visual representations (such as diagrams, sketches, or wireframes) used to communicate design ideas and structure.
  • Sitemap is a diagram that shows how different pages and sections of a website are organized and connected.

docs.title: Content Hierarchy Principles
docs.text:
Content hierarchy helps present information in a clear and meaningful order. It typically involves:

  1. Prioritizing important information – placing key content where users will notice it first.
  2. Organizing content logically – grouping related information together so users can easily understand how sections are connected.
  3. Guiding user attention – using headings, layout, and structure to naturally lead users through the content.

docs.title: Navigation Systems Structure
docs.text:
Navigation systems are designed to help users move through content smoothly. They typically include:

  1. Clear pathways – allowing users to easily move between different sections.
  2. Consistency – keeping navigation elements uniform across pages to avoid confusion.
  3. Ease of use – ensuring users can quickly find what they are looking for with minimal effort.

docs.title: In-class Activities
docs.text:
These are the in-class activities completed during the session.

The first activity focused on organizing content hierarchy, where we structured information to improve clarity and usability. This helped us understand how important it is to present information based on user needs.

The second activity involved creating a sitemap, where we mapped out the structure of a website and showed how different pages are connected. This emphasized the role of navigation and labeling in guiding users.

We also created supporting artifacts such as diagrams and layouts to visually represent our ideas.

This activity was completed in a group. Our group consisted of:

  • Ryan Bo
  • Long
  • Calvin

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: This is the report of the event that took place in ACIT 2811 - UX/UI. The class focused on prototyping, where we explored how ideas begin to take shape through visual representations. We learned how to map user interactions and create simple mockups to test and communicate design concepts.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
The main topics discussed in class were:

  1. Wireframes
  2. User Flows
  3. Artifacts
  4. User Flow Diagrams
  5. Low Fidelity Mockups

docs.title: Definition of Topics
docs.text:
• Wireframes are basic visual layouts that outline the structure of a page or interface without detailed design elements.

  • User Flows represent the path a user takes to complete a specific task within a product or system.

  • Artifacts are visual outputs such as sketches, diagrams, or mockups used to communicate design ideas.

  • User Flow Diagrams are visual representations that map out each step a user takes when interacting with a system.

  • Low Fidelity Mockups are simple, rough versions of a design that focus on layout and functionality rather than visual details.

docs.title: Wireframing Process
docs.text:
Wireframing is used to establish the basic structure of a design before adding visual detail. It typically involves:

  1. Defining layout structure – arranging key elements such as headers, content areas, and navigation.

  2. Focusing on functionality – emphasizing how users will interact with the interface rather than how it looks.

  3. Iterating quickly – making rapid changes to explore different layout options and improve usability.

docs.title: User Flow Development
docs.text:
User flows help designers understand and improve how users complete tasks. This process typically includes:

  1. Identifying user goals – determining what the user wants to accomplish.

  2. Mapping steps – outlining each action the user takes to reach their goal.

  3. Analyzing efficiency – reviewing the flow to remove unnecessary steps and improve the experience.

docs.title: In-class Activities
docs.text:
These are the in-class activities completed during the session.

The first activity focused on creating user flows, where we mapped out the steps a user takes to complete a specific task. This helped us better understand how users interact with a system and identify areas for improvement.

The second activity involved developing wireframes and low fidelity mockups, where we translated our ideas into simple visual layouts. These mockups focused on structure and usability rather than detailed design.

We also created user flow diagrams and other supporting artifacts to clearly communicate our design process and ideas.

This activity was completed in a group. Our group consisted of:

  • Ryan Bo
  • Long
  • Calvin

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text:
• Wireframe Creation
  • User Flow Diagram Development
  • Low Fidelity Prototype Design
